texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Evidence continues to mount that the enhanced greenhouse effect is caused by increased emissions of infrared light absorbing components. Carbon dioxide is the largest contributor as a result of the large amounts emitted in power dioxide from various feed gas streams. Membrane gas absorption (MGA) using dedicated absorption liquids (CORAL) in conjunction with porous polypropylene hollow fibre membranes for carbon dioxide removal is discussed in this paper. Mass transfer results achieved in a pilot plant, including regeneration of the absorption liquids, are presented. The impact of carbon dioxide partial pressure, liquid loading and liquid temperature on the carbon dioxide membrane flux is discussed and compared with literature data for other systems. The novel absorption liquids show an excellent performance in terms of system stability and mass transfer, when used in combination with commercially available, inexpensive polyolefin membranes. The largest application under development is the production of carbon dioxide for the horticultural industry. Amongst the spin-off activities the life support applications are prevalent. © 2002 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.orig: The essential element in a membrane absorber is a porous, water repellent, polymeric membrane. The gas phase remains separated from the liquid absorbent as a result of the hydrophobicity of the membrane. A flue gas stream is fed along one side of the membrane. The components to be removed from the gas stream will diffuse through the gas filled pores of the membrane. On the other side of the membrane they will be absorbed into the absorption liquid. texts.orig: The choice of a suitable combination of absorption liquid and membrane is very important. The hydrophobicity and pore characteristics of the membrane material should be such that the pores remain gas-filled at an excess pressure of preferably more than 1 bar on the liquid side. Commercially available porous polyolefin membranes appear not to be suitable for operation with the usual absorption liquids, i.e. aqueous solutions of ethanolamines [3-6]. In the course of time such liquids will seep through these membranes as a result of the low surface tension of the organic liquid / water mixtures. This can be resolved by the utilisation of expensive PTFE membranes [7]. An alternative solution to this is the use of an absorp tion liquid of surface tension similar to water. TNO is currently developing the membrane gas absorption process for acid gas removal based on a patented working method [8] using dedicated absorption liquids (CORAL) based on mixtures of salts and amino-acids and polyolefin membranes. This paves the way towards the use of inexpensive polyolefin membranes, developed for, e.g. medical applications (artificial lungs), which is believed to be essential for economic success of membrane gas absorption. The use of salt solutions also has the advantage that the vapour pressure of the active components is zero and the solvent is not lost through evaporation.

texts.orig: The membrane absorber design cannot be based on conventional membrane module designs. These designs have historically been based on the use of hollow fibre membranes for filtration duties with ill-defined flow conditions on the shell side of the membrane. When membranes are used for contacting duties, as is the case for membrane gas absorption, it is necessary that flow conditions be well defined on either side of the membrane to achieve good mass transfer. Furthermore, it is essential that, given the large volume flows of flue gas streams, the module design is easy to scale-up. TNO has patented a transversal flow membrane module design, shown in Fig. 3, which has good mass transfer characteristics and good scale-up potential.

TNO has patented a transversal flow membrane module design, shown in Fig. 3, which has good mass transfer characteristics and good scale-up potential.

For CO2-MGA using ethanolamines as the absorption liquid the reaction data and equilibrium data are well-documented and mass transfer modelling is straightforward. As mentioned before ethanolamines cannot be used in conjunction with polyolefin membranes, which sparked off the development of novel absorption liquids. texts.orig: pattern is cross-flow but overall the flow pattern is counter-current because the membrane modules consists of a number of elements. The rich liquid is fed to the top of a stripper via a plate heat exchanger. The stripper temperature is maintained at 105 °C by an electric heating element. As a result of the temperature increase of the absorption liquid CO2 is liberated from the solution. Water is recovered in the condenser and trickles back into the stripper. The lean liquid exits the stripper at the bottom and is fed back to the membrane absorber via the heat exchanger. Temperatures, pressures, dew points, flow rates and carbon dioxide concentrations at inlet and outlet of the membrane absorber have been measured continuously and are used for the performance assessment. During all experiments, air was used as the main feed gas component, which did not result in a deterioration in performance over a discontinuous four months testing period using the same absorption liquid. This illustrates that the CORAL liquid used has an edge in terms of chemical stability over, e.g. mono-ethanolamine, which degrades in the presence of oxygen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mass transfer performance, expressed as a carbon dioxide flux through the membrane, is shown in Fig. 5 for the CORAL20 liquid (2 M of active components) at room temperature as a function of the logarithmic average carbon dioxide partial pressure. The data have been gathered from a variety of experiments at different gas and liquid flow rates, resulting in gas flow velocities between 0.05 and 0.45 m / s and liquid flow velocities between 0.7 and 30 mm / s. Carbon dioxide partial pressures are typical for flue gas and life support applications. Cyclic loadings were up to 0.11 mol CO2 / mol of active component, which is much lower than the theoretically expected loading of 0.45 mol / mol. The lean liquid loading at the absorber inlet was, typically, around 0.055 mol CO2 / mol of active component.

texts.orig: The measured CO2-fluxes show a near linear increase at low CO 2 partial pressure, with a tendency to level off towards an asymptotic limit at higher CO2 partial pressure. At low CO2 partial pressure the behaviour is described by the fast reaction regime with an overall mass transfer coefficient of 1.6 × 10 -3 m / s, which was derived from the tangent line at the axis. This can be understood by the fact that at low carbon dioxide partial pressure the concentration of the active components in the liquid is much larger than the CO2-concentration in the liquid at the interface. A mass transfer analysis was carried out using the mass transfer theory given in Section 3. Estimates for the mass transfer coefficient in the gas phase and the membrane were based on equations Eqs. (3)-(7). The liquid side mass transfer coefficient was derived from Eq. (2) using the other mass transfer coefficient estimates. Results are shown in Table 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mass transfer coefficients are influenced by many variables, such as temperature, CO 2 partial pressure and liquid loading. This makes comparison of experimental results of MGA-systems from various literature sources nearly impossible. Nevertheless, the results in Table 4 illustrate that the CORAL MGA system gives the highest mass transfer coefficient, despite the lower concentration of active components (2 vs. 5 M). Furthermore, the literature data were obtained from experiments in which the liquid was fed through the membrane modules and then discarded. Hence, the solvent did not contain CO2 at the absorber inlet. In contrast with this, the results of this study were obtained in a system in which the liquid was regenerated continuously and re-used. Hence, it gives a good representation of the performance in an actual plant. As the performance for the CORAL MGA-system was determined for 2 M concentrations of the active components, it can be expected that the mass transfer performance can be improved upon.

texts.orig: 6. Applications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The potential of this technology has been identified in a number of assessment studies for different applications. The ensuing customer-driven development has been focusing on the production of carbon dioxide from flue gas with the aim of supplying high purity carbon dioxide to the horticultural industry to increase crop yield [9,15,16]. Other commercial applications are, for instance, in the field of life support [17-20]. Fig. 8 shows an overview of the atmospheric pressure applications currently being investigated.
texts.text: The potential of this technology has been identified in a number of assessment studies for different applications. The ensuing customer-driven development has been focusing on the production of carbon dioxide from flue gas with the aim of supplying high purity carbon dioxide to the horticultural industry to increase crop yield [9,15,16]. Other commercial applications are, for instance, in the field of life support [17-20]. Fig. 8 shows an overview of the atmospheric pressure applications currently being investigated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The largest application, currently under development, is the production of CO 2 from flue gases from gas-fired cogeneration plants and re-use in greenhouses. In the Netherlands carbon dioxide is used to promote plant growth in greenhouses. Depending on the crop, the production can be increased by 25% by increasing the CO 2 concentration to 500 ppm inside the greenhouse. It is necessary to heat these greenhouses during cold periods, which makes the horticultural industry in the Netherlands a large consumer of natural gas. Future heat supply systems will be based largely on cogeneration plants as significant energy savings can be achieved. Energy savings can be increased if the heat supply is coupled to carbon dioxide supply. This will incite growers to buy heat from the cogeneration plant rather than to use their own boilers. These boilers are needed to cover peak demand and emergencies. The heat demand and CO2 demand are generally anticyclic. During periods of high heat demand (dur- ing winter and at night) little or no CO 2 is needed to maintain the desired CO2 levels in the greenhouse. During periods of high CO 2 demand (during summer and during the day) little or no heat is needed. The thermal energy of the cogeneration plant can either be used for greenhouse heating or for the production of carbon dioxide. This will

texts.orig: 7. Conclusions
texts.text: 7. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas absorption based on the novel absorption liquids (CORAL) and porous polyolefin membranes is an efficient technique for removal of carbon dioxide from various feed gases. The applications currently under development underline the industrial relevance of this new gas treating process.
texts.text: Membrane gas absorption based on the novel absorption liquids (CORAL) and porous polyolefin membranes is an efficient technique for removal of carbon dioxide from various feed gases. The applications currently under development underline the industrial relevance of this new gas treating process.
